43: A direct revelation: 'To him all the prophets bear witness that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name.' Peter's summary of the OT witness to Christ is comprehensive ('all the prophets') and universalizing ('everyone who believes'). The Cornelius episode's Christological climax is precisely this: the same Christ who is the subject of all prophetic testimony is the one through whom Gentiles receive forgiveness. The Spirit's falling on the Gentiles mid-sermon (Acts 10:44) is God's own validation of this Christological universalism — the risen Christ's lordship over all flesh (Joel 2:28) enacted in real time.
1: Cornelius's story begins in Caesarea Maritima — not Jerusalem. The mission's expansion to the uncircumcised begins not in the holy city but in the Roman capital of Judea, in the household of a Roman military officer. This geographical inversion is christologically significant: Jesus had said his witnesses would go 'to the ends of the earth' (Acts 1:8), and the Gentile mission's beginning with a Roman centurion in a Roman capital previews the trajectory that ends in Rome itself (Acts 28). The Lord of all (Acts 10:36) reaches all by beginning at the geographic center of Gentile power in Judea.
2: Cornelius is described as 'devout and God-fearing with all his household, who gave alms generously to the people and prayed continually to God.' He is everything a Gentile could be while remaining outside the covenant. His prayer and almsgiving are precisely the practices Jesus had elevated in the Sermon (Matthew 6:1–18) as the marks of authentic piety. The God-fearer who does what Jesus praised in the sermon is now the one whom the Lord reaches first in the Gentile mission — a vindication of Jesus's teaching that such practices indicate a heart already oriented toward God.
3: The angel's vision at the ninth hour (3 p.m.) — the Jewish hour of prayer and the hour of the afternoon sacrifice — connects Cornelius to Israel's prayer rhythms even as a Gentile. The angel's appearance echoes the angel at the tomb (Luke 24:4) and the angel who released the apostles from prison (Acts 5:19). Divine messengers consistently appear to advance the mission of the risen Christ; Cornelius's angel is the divine initiative preceding and enabling his encounter with the gospel.
4: 'Your prayers and your alms have ascended as a memorial before God' — the language of the memorial offering (azkarah, LXX: mnēmosynon) applied to Cornelius's prayers and alms echoes Leviticus 2:2 and was used for offerings that 'rose before God' in the temple cult. The risen Christ's God is attentive to the prayers of a Gentile whose heart is oriented toward him — the temple system's memorial offering is replaced by the memorial of faithful prayer, accessible across ethnic and geographical boundaries.
5: The angel's instruction to send for Simon Peter at Joppa — not for a priest, not for a teacher of the law, but for an apostle of Jesus Christ — establishes that the answer to Cornelius's prayer is the gospel, not a conversion to Judaism. What Cornelius needs is not Moses but Christ. The specific name and address ('Simon who is called Peter,' at the house of Simon the tanner, by the sea) are the angel's detailed instruction — divine precision directing the Gentile inquirer to the apostolic witness.
6: Peter is staying with Simon the tanner — a point of significant contact purity: tanners worked with animal hides and were considered impure in Jewish law, their homes unfit for observant Jews to stay in. Peter's already-loosened purity practice is the setting for the rooftop vision that will follow. The risen Christ is progressively moving his apostle away from purity barriers before requiring him to cross the greatest barrier — entering a Gentile home.
7: Cornelius sends two servants and a devout soldier — the household's coordination in responding to the divine instruction. The household as a unit of obedience mirrors the household conversion pattern that the Cornelius episode will establish: when the head responds to God, the household moves with him. The 'devout soldier' (stratiōtēn eusebē) — a God-fearer himself — demonstrates that Cornelius's piety had formed the spiritual atmosphere of his entire household before Peter arrives.
8: The dispatch of the messengers to Joppa begins the convergence of two simultaneous divine actions: the angel working with Cornelius in Caesarea, the Spirit working with Peter in Joppa. The Cornelius story is structured as a double vision — Gentile and Jewish, Caesarea and Joppa, centurion and apostle — divinely coordinated so that neither party's encounter is accidental or human-initiated. This is the pattern of the risen Christ's mission: he orchestrates both sides of the encounter.
9: Peter goes up on the roof to pray at the sixth hour (noon) — the midday prayer time. His practice of regular prayer at fixed hours connects to the same rhythm that had brought Cornelius to pray at the ninth hour (Acts 10:3). The two men are being prepared for their encounter through the same practice of prayer, each in their own setting, each receiving divine instruction that will bring them together. Prayer is the posture in which both men become available to the risen Lord's direction.
10: Peter becomes hungry and falls into a trance (ekstasis) — an altered state of consciousness in which divine revelation comes. The trance comes on the body's natural hunger, which the vision will address in an unexpected way: rather than satisfying Peter's hunger with ordinary food, God uses the hunger moment to address the hunger for the Gentile mission that the gospel has been building toward. The body's need becomes the occasion for the soul's instruction.
11: The vision: heaven opened and something like a great sheet descending, held by four corners, lowered to earth. The 'opened heaven' is the standard theophanic frame (Ezekiel 1:1; Mark 1:10; Acts 7:56; Revelation 4:1) — the boundary between divine and human spheres is momentarily permeable. The sheet coming down from heaven to earth enacts what the gospel declares: the holy descends to the unclean, not to contaminate but to cleanse. The direction of movement is the direction of the incarnation itself.
12: All kinds of animals — 'all kinds of four-footed animals and reptiles and birds of the air' — represented the full range of the Mosaic clean/unclean animal taxonomy. The vision's comprehensiveness is deliberate: the dietary laws were the most concrete, daily marker of Jewish-Gentile distinction. Jesus had already taught that what enters the mouth does not defile (Mark 7:14–23), and Mark's Gospel explicitly noted: 'Thus he declared all foods clean' (Acts 7:19). Peter's rooftop vision is the Spirit's application of Jesus's teaching to Peter's resistant practice.
13: The divine voice commands: 'Rise, Peter; kill and eat.' The command is not an invitation to dietary violation but a divine annulment of the clean/unclean distinction — the same annulment Jesus had made in principle (Mark 7:19). The command also connects to Peter's role as apostle: his task is not merely to receive the vision but to act on it. 'Rise' (anastas) — the same verb used for resurrection appearances — frames Peter's response to the divine command as itself a kind of rising.
14: 'By no means, Lord; for I have never eaten anything that is common or unclean.' Peter's refusal echoes his earlier objection to Jesus's foot-washing: 'You shall never wash my feet' (John 13:8). In both cases, Peter's piety leads him to resist the Lord's initiative. In both cases, the Lord overrides the objection: 'What I have made clean, do not call common' (Acts 10:15); 'If I do not wash you, you have no share with me' (John 13:8). The pattern of Peter's well-intentioned resistance to the Lord's purifying initiative is consistent across the Gospels and into Acts.
15: 'What God has made clean, do not call common' — the divine declaration is the theological crux of the vision. The distinction between clean and unclean was Mosaic law; the revocation of that distinction is divine prerogative, and it comes from the God of Moses himself. The Lord who gave the food laws is the Lord who revokes their boundary function — not because the food laws were wrong, but because they were types pointing toward the real distinction (holy/unholy, life/death) that Christ has now definitively addressed in his death and resurrection.
16: The three-fold repetition of the vision — the sheet descends, the command comes, Peter refuses, the sheet ascends, repeated three times — mirrors Peter's three-fold denial of Jesus (Luke 22:54–62) and his three-fold restoration (John 21:15–17). Peter who denied the Lord three times is now confronted with the Lord's command three times before full obedience. The number three in Peter's narrative is never accidental; Luke is connecting dots across the Gospel-Acts sequence.
17: Peter is 'inwardly perplexed' (diaporeō) about the vision's meaning while Cornelius's men arrive at the gate — the divine timing is precise. The double action of Spirit (Acts 10:19-20) and circumstance (the men's arrival) together constitute the interpretation: the vision is about these men, about Cornelius, about the Gentile mission. The risen Lord does not explain the vision abstractly; he provides the living interpretation through the next step of the story.
18: The men ask whether Simon Peter is staying there — the precise address the angel gave to Cornelius. The angel's specific information — street address, host's name — enabled this precise inquiry. The risen Christ who told his disciples the exact location of the upper room (Luke 22:10–12) and the donkey for his entry (Luke 19:29–31) continues to work through precise divine instructions that bring his people to exactly the right place at exactly the right time.
19: While Peter is pondering the vision, the Spirit speaks explicitly: 'Behold, three men are looking for you.' The Spirit who fell at Pentecost (Acts 2:4), who spoke through the prophets (Acts 1:16), who guided Philip to the Ethiopian (Acts 8:29), now directly addresses Peter — the apostle who is slowest to move on his own initiative receives the most explicit divine guidance. The Spirit's initiative compensates for Peter's reluctance, ensuring that the Gentile mission opens not through the apostle's theological innovation but through unmistakable divine directive.
20: 'Rise and go down and accompany them without hesitation, for I have sent them.' The Spirit's command uses the same authority formula as Jesus's mission commands: 'I have sent them.' The risen Lord's authority is exercised through the Spirit; the Spirit's commissions are the risen Lord's commissions. Peter is being sent not by human imagination but by the divine initiative that sent Jesus ('As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you,' John 20:21).
21: Peter goes down and identifies himself — the apostle of the Lord meeting the messengers of the centurion. His announcement, 'I am the one you are looking for,' echoes Jesus's self-identification before his arrest ('I am he,' John 18:5–6) and the divine name (ego eimi). Peter's willingness to identify himself rather than send them away marks the beginning of his obedience to the Spirit's explicit command. The meeting at the gate is the hinge between the old and new phases of the mission.
22: Cornelius's credentials — 'righteous and God-fearing, well spoken of by the whole Jewish nation' — are presented by his messengers to Peter. The testimony creates the moral context: Peter is not being asked to associate with wickedness but to cross the ethnoreligious boundary to reach a man whose heart is already oriented toward God. Jesus had praised the centurion of Capernaum as having greater faith than anyone in Israel (Luke 7:9); Cornelius continues the pattern of Roman centurions who prove more spiritually receptive than expected.
23: Peter invites the men in and hosts them overnight — his first act of Gentile hospitality, crossing the purity barrier before even reaching Cornelius's house. The risen Lord's mission begins dismantling the barriers one step at a time: host Gentile messengers tonight, visit a Gentile house tomorrow. Peter's hospitality is itself a sign that the vision is already working in him, preparing him for the more radical hospitality of entering the centurion's home.
24: Cornelius has assembled 'his relatives and close friends' to hear Peter — a household gathering that mirrors the communal character of early church meetings. Cornelius acts on his divine instruction before Peter arrives: he has gathered an audience for the gospel he has not yet heard. This proactive faith — assembling to receive a word not yet spoken — is exactly what Jesus praised in the centurion of Capernaum: 'only say the word, and my servant will be healed' (Luke 7:7). Trust in the Lord's word precedes its audible reception.
25: Cornelius falls at Peter's feet in worship (prosekyneō). The gesture of prostration before an apostle is immediately corrected — just as it will be corrected before an angel in Revelation 19:10; Acts 22:8–9. The prohibition of worship of any agent other than God is consistent with the risen Lord's own expectation: 'Worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve' (Luke 4:8). The gospel does not create a new hierarchy of mediating figures to be venerated; it clears the path to the one Mediator.
26: 'Stand up; I too am a man' — Peter's refusal of worship is the anti-Herod moment. Herod Agrippa's acceptance of divine acclamation will cost him his life (Acts 12:23); Peter's rejection of it exemplifies the apostolic self-understanding that the glory of the risen Lord is not to be transferred to his servants. The apostles point away from themselves to the one in whose name they act — consistent with Jesus's own pattern of directing attention to the Father rather than to himself (John 5:30; Acts 7:16–18).
27: The conversation as Peter enters (Acts 10:27a) leads to the assembled crowd (Acts 10:27b) — the private approach becoming a public audience. The gathering is the fulfillment of Cornelius's obedient preparation (Acts 10:24). Peter's entrance into a Gentile home — which he narrates in 11:12 as the moment of explicit Spirit-command — is the physical crossing of the boundary that the vision had prepared him for. The body crossing the threshold enacts what the mind had only pondered.
28: 'You yourselves know how unlawful it is for a Jew to associate with or to visit anyone of another nation' — Peter's acknowledgment of the law he is breaking establishes the stakes. The Jewish purity laws against Gentile association were not merely social convention but halakhic practice with deep scriptural roots. His next clause — 'but God has shown me that I should not call any person common or unclean' — extends the rooftop vision from food (the vision's content) to persons (the vision's application). The logic is explicit: if God has made all foods clean, he has made all persons accessible.
29: Peter asks why Cornelius sent for him — not because he needs to know before obeying, but because the divine orchestration needs to be heard publicly. The full story of God's initiative through the angel and through the rooftop vision must be narrated so that the gathered household can understand that their encounter with the gospel is itself divinely initiated. The risen Lord's presence in the story is established before the gospel content is proclaimed.
30: Cornelius's account of the angelic vision — four days ago, at the ninth hour of prayer, a man in bright clothing — establishes the divine parallel to Peter's rooftop vision. The 'man in bright clothing' (anēr en esthēti lampra) echoes the description of the angels at the empty tomb (Luke 24:4 — 'two men in dazzling apparel'). The resurrection's emissaries are recognizable by their brightness; the Gentile centurion has already been visited by the same glorious presence that announced Jesus's resurrection to the women.
31: The angel's message — 'your prayer has been heard and your alms have been remembered before God' — echoes the angel's words in Acts 10:4. The repetition is deliberate: Luke wants the full assembly to hear the divine confirmation that Cornelius's pre-Christian piety was genuinely received before God. The God who raised Jesus is the God who was already attentive to a Gentile's prayer and almsgiving. The gospel does not reach an indifferent heart but one God has already been drawing.
32: The instruction to send for Simon Peter in Joppa — repeated now in Cornelius's account — is the second telling of the divine directive. The gospel of Jesus Christ reaches Cornelius not by accident, by missionary initiative, or by Cornelius's seeking, but by explicit divine direction. The risen Lord's mission strategy includes the orchestration of encounters through angelic intervention — the same divine initiative that directed Philip to the Ethiopian (Acts 8:26) and Paul to Macedonia (Acts 16:9).
33: 'We are all here in the presence of God to hear all that you have been commanded by the Lord.' Cornelius's statement of readiness — 'in the presence of God' (enōpion tou theou) — is the theological framing that transforms a household meeting into a divine encounter. The assembled Gentiles are not passive recipients of human teaching but active participants in a divinely orchestrated audience with the word of God. The risen Lord has prepared both speaker and hearers before the word is spoken.
34: 'Truly I understand that God shows no partiality' (ouk estin prosōpolēmptēs ho theos) — Peter's opening declaration is the theological conclusion to which the rooftop vision and the Cornelius encounter have been pointing. The Greek prosōpolēmptēs (person-respecter) is a word coined to translate the Hebrew nasa panim ('lift the face,' the OT term for favoritism in judgment). Jesus had modeled this impartiality: 'he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good' (Matthew 5:45); he healed Gentiles (Luke 7:1–10; Acts 17:11–19) and declared the faith of a Syrophoenician woman (Mark 7:29). Peter is catching up to what Jesus had demonstrated.
35: 'But in every nation anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him' — the criterion for divine acceptance is not ethnic identity but the fear of God and righteous practice. This does not teach that sincerity saves apart from Christ (the rest of the sermon makes Christ the exclusive Savior); it teaches that the God who saves through Christ has been drawing toward himself persons from every nation whose hearts are oriented toward him. Cornelius's pre-gospel piety was acceptable as evidence of God's prior work in him; the gospel now provides what his seeking was pointing toward.
36: 'As for the word that he sent to Israel, preaching good news of peace through Jesus Christ — he is Lord of all' (houtos estin pantōn kyrios). The climax of the declaration comes in three words: Lord of all (pantōn kyrios). Jesus's lordship is not limited to Israel, not bounded by ethnic or national lines. The word sent to Israel through Jesus — the gospel first proclaimed in Galilee and Jerusalem — is now declared to have universal scope. The Lord whose resurrection Peter witnessed is the Lord of Cornelius's household, of Caesar's household, of every nation.
37: 'You yourselves know what happened throughout all Judea, beginning from Galilee after the baptism that John proclaimed' — Peter appeals to Cornelius's existing knowledge. As a Roman officer stationed in Judea, Cornelius would have been aware of the Jesus movement as a public phenomenon. The summary of Jesus's ministry ('you know') places Peter's proclamation in continuity with public history, not private mythology. The gospel is about things that happened — datable, locatable, observable events that Roman officials like Cornelius could investigate.
38: 'How God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power' — the language of anointing (echrisen) is christological: 'Christ' means 'anointed one.' Luke 4:18–21 had shown Jesus applying Isaiah 61 to himself ('The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me'). Peter's summary 'God anointed Jesus with the Holy Spirit and power' is the apostolic confirmation of Jesus's self-proclamation in Nazareth. The Jesus who went about healing and delivering is the Anointed One whom Isaiah foretold and whose anointing was enacted at his baptism (Luke 3:22).
39: 'And we are witnesses of all that he did both in the country of the Jews and in Jerusalem. They put him to death by hanging him on a tree.' The apostolic witness formula — 'we are witnesses' (hēmeis martyres) — appears at every major proclamation in Acts (Acts 2:32; Acts 3:15; Acts 5:32; Acts 13:31). The summary of Jesus's ministry and death compresses the Gospel narrative into a single declaration accessible to Gentiles: he did good works throughout Judea (Acts 10:38), they crucified him (Acts 10:39). 'Hanging on a tree' (kremasantes epi xylou) invokes Deuteronomy 21:23 — the curse-bearing death that Paul expounds in Galatians 3:13.
40: 'But God raised him on the third day and made him to appear' — the resurrection as God's act (not Jesus raising himself) is consistent with Peter's proclamation throughout Acts (Acts 2:24, 32; Acts 3:15, 26; Acts 4:10). The 'third day' is the specific chronological marker that connects to Jesus's own predictions of his resurrection (Luke 9:22; Acts 18:33; Acts 24:46) and to Paul's earliest creedal formula (1 Corinthians 15:4). 'Made him to appear' (edōken auton emphanē genesthai) — the resurrection appearances are divine gifts, arranged by God for his chosen witnesses.
41: 'Not to all the people but to us who had been chosen by God as witnesses, who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead' — the specificity of the resurrection appearances (chosen witnesses, not public spectacle) is both historical (the risen Jesus appeared to named individuals in identifiable circumstances) and theological (the witness function requires commissioned testifiers, not public demonstration). 'We ate and drank with him after he rose' — the table fellowship of the risen Lord (Luke 24:30–31, 41–43) is the apostolic experiential verification of bodily resurrection.
42: 'And he commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that he is the one appointed by God to be judge of the living and the dead.' The risen Lord's commission to preach includes the declaration of his judicial role — he is the judge of all humanity. The Great Commission (Matthew 28:18–20; Luke 24:47–48; Acts 1:8) includes not only the offer of forgiveness but the declaration of judgment. The same Jesus who forgives through faith is the judge of those who do not believe — a truth Peter holds together with the offer of forgiveness in the next verse.
44: 'While Peter was still saying these things, the Holy Spirit fell on all who heard the word' — the Spirit falls before Peter finishes his sermon, before the invitation is extended, before any prayer for the Spirit is offered, before baptism is administered. The Spirit's timing is sovereign: not waiting for human completion but breaking in at the divine moment. This is the Gentile Pentecost, and like the Jewish Pentecost, it is entirely God's initiative. Peter is interrupted by the Spirit he is describing — the sermon becomes its own fulfillment.
45: 'The believers from among the circumcised who had come with Peter were amazed' (existēmi) — the same verb used for the Pentecost crowd's amazement (Acts 2:7). Jewish believers witnessing the Spirit fall on Gentiles experience the same cognitive shock as the Jerusalem crowd experiencing the Spirit fall on Jews. The 'gift of the Holy Spirit' (hē dōrea tou hagiou pneumatos) is the term used in 2:38 for the Spirit received at baptism — but here it comes before and independent of baptism, on uncircumcised Gentiles, while the apostle is preaching. The Spirit's freedom from human ritual ordering is the theological provocation.
46: 'For they were hearing them speaking in tongues and extolling God' — the evidence of the Spirit's presence is identical to Pentecost: tongues and praise. The same Spirit, the same signs, the same God. The Gentile Pentecost is not a different gift with different signs but the same Spirit whose outpouring Peter had proclaimed at Pentecost: 'the promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off, everyone whom the Lord our God calls to himself' (Acts 2:39). Cornelius's household is the first 'far off' fulfillment of that promise.
47: 'Can anyone withhold water for baptizing these people, who have received the Holy Spirit just as we have?' — Peter's question makes the logic explicit: the Spirit's reception is the divine acceptance of these Gentiles; water baptism is the human confirmation of divine decision. The Spirit's prior action removes all grounds for withholding the covenant sign. The argument structure is the same as Paul's in Galatians 3:2: 'Did you receive the Spirit by works of the law or by hearing with faith?' If the Spirit has come by faith apart from circumcision, circumcision cannot be required for full inclusion.
48: Peter commands baptism in the name of Jesus Christ — the same formula as Pentecost (Acts 2:38) applied to Gentiles. The continuity of baptismal practice across the Jewish-Gentile boundary is the institutional enactment of Peter's theological conclusion ('God shows no partiality'). They ask Peter to remain for some days — the host-guest relationship inverted: the Jewish apostle staying in the Gentile home, eating at the Gentile table, extended for days. The mission that began with Peter's reluctance to enter a Gentile house ends with his lingering as a guest.
